briefs.lessonTitle: Welcome: winning and keeping school & childcare floor-care work
briefs.script: Welcome aboard. This course is for Australian commercial cleaning operators, technicians and small business owners who want to win and reliably service floor-care work in schools and childcare centres. You may already have plenty of cleaning experience. What this course adds is the compliance, child-safety and documentation layer that separates a casual job from a contract a client keeps renewing. We work through four jobs: carpet cleaning, tile and grout, vinyl strip and seal, and pressure washing. Here is the thesis. A school client is not just buying a clean floor. They are buying a contractor who can show current Safety Data Sheets, choose lower-tox products, keep children away from wet or treated areas, and produce the paperwork. Just so you know, this is professional development and contract-readiness training. It is not IICRC accredited and does not award continuing education credits. We are also careful with numbers. We will not give fixed dry, cure or re-entry times, or quote slip ratings, because those depend on the product or the place. Module zero lays the foundation. Work through the lessons in order, and finish with a short knowledge check.

briefs.lessonTitle: Your WHS duties: current SDS, the chemicals register & labelling
briefs.script: In this lesson we set out your work health and safety duties, so you know what a contract expects before you open a container. Floor-care work means handling hazardous chemicals, and those come with legal duties. Australia has a model WHS Act and Regulations, but the model laws only take effect once a state, territory or the Commonwealth adopts them. The exception to flag is Victoria, which operates under its own OHS Act and Regulations. So for a Victorian contract, work to OHS and check with WorkSafe Victoria. Now the core duties. If you handle hazardous chemicals, you must keep a current Safety Data Sheet for each one, accessible to workers. Current means not more than five years old, and the sheet shows its last-reviewed date. You must also maintain a hazardous chemicals register under model regulation 346. Labelling matters too, including any container you decant into. Pour a stripper into a spray bottle, and that bottle still needs a correct label. One point of confusion is the manifest. That is only required above the Schedule eleven thresholds, which floor-care quantities do not reach. Next, we read an SDS and build the register.

briefs.lessonTitle: How to read a Safety Data Sheet (and build your register)
briefs.script: In this lesson we read a Safety Data Sheet and assemble those sheets into a working register. An SDS is the most useful safety document you carry. It is structured, with the same headings in roughly the same order on every product, so it is quick to scan. The sections you lean on most are hazards, handling and storage, PPE, first aid and disposal. Hazards are communicated through the Globally Harmonised System, or GHS, which sorts chemicals into hazard classes and shows them with standard pictograms, like the corrosion symbol. Let us make it real. Actichem Instastrip, SDS code AP400, is a vinyl-floor stripper, so its SDS confirms the hazard class, PPE and ventilation. Compare that with a carpet prespray, Actichem Clean Force, SDS AP453. The SDS and technical data sheet are also where product-specific numbers live, like dilution. We do not give fixed figures, because they vary, so always follow the product SDS and TDS. To build the register, list every hazardous chemical, attach a current SDS, keep it accessible, and review it whenever you add a product. And if an item has no current SDS, do not guess. Obtain it from the supplier first.

briefs.lessonTitle: Choosing child-safe, low-tox products and ventilating occupied rooms
briefs.script: In this lesson we cover why child-safe selection matters, and the practical steps to clean responsibly in occupied rooms. Schools and especially childcare centres are sensitive environments. Children spend long hours near the floors, they are physically closer to surfaces, and they are more vulnerable to chemical exposure and infection. The why comes from the National Quality Framework, overseen by ACECQA. The part most relevant to you is Quality Area two, children's health and safety, which covers protecting children from harm and from infection. When you help a centre control infection risk and avoid unnecessary exposure, you directly help them meet Quality Area two. So how do you put it into practice? Select lower-tox products suited to occupied spaces, and read the SDS first. Dilute per the label, no stronger to be sure. Ventilate by bringing in fresh air while you work and while products dry. And isolate wet or treated areas, signing the zone and keeping children out until it is genuinely ready. Because re-entry timing is product-specific, follow the product SDS and TDS. Schedule heavier jobs into deep-clean or holiday programs. And remember, child-related work generally requires a Working With Children check.

briefs.lessonTitle: Knowledge check: foundations
briefs.script: Let us pause and check the foundations from Module zero. This is a short self-check covering your WHS duties, reading a Safety Data Sheet and building a register, and choosing child-safe products for occupied rooms. Work through each question and pick the single best answer before you look at the key. Treat it as a way to find any gaps rather than a test to pass. If a question makes you hesitate, that is useful. Go back to the lesson it came from, read that section again, then return and try once more. When you are confident across all of these, you are ready to move on to the next module. Have a go now.

briefs.lessonTitle: Carpet, fibre and soil: an S100-aligned overview
briefs.script: Let's start with what you're actually cleaning and the framework professionals work to. For textile floor coverings, the recognised standard of care is ANSI/IICRC S100. It pulls together chemistry, fibre and construction knowledge, soil management, equipment, methods, safety, and pre and post inspection, and it deliberately allows for more than one cleaning method. Keep one thing clear, though. The IICRC is an industry certification body, not a government regulator. S100 is a respected standard of care that supports your judgement. It isn't law, and it isn't mandatory the way a WHS regulation is. The fibre and the way the carpet is built shape every decision, so identify what's in front of you and match your method to it. Soil isn't one thing either. It ranges from dry grit through to oily residues and water-soluble spills, and a thorough vacuum removes much of it before any wet process. Pre-inspection is part of the job. It lets you read the fibre, the soil load and any existing damage, and it protects you commercially. Next, we'll weigh hot water extraction against encapsulation.

briefs.lessonTitle: Hot water extraction vs encapsulation: the dry-time trade-off
briefs.script: Because S100 allows more than one method, the two you'll weigh most often in schools and childcare are hot water extraction and encapsulation. Neither is simply better. They sit at different points on a trade-off between how much soil you remove and how quickly the carpet returns to service. Hot water extraction applies cleaning solution and then extracts it, along with suspended soil and moisture, in the same pass. Its strength is depth, which suits heavily soiled carpet or a periodic deep clean. The trade-off is more moisture, so the surface needs longer before it's back in use, and how long depends on the carpet and the conditions on the day. Encapsulation is a low-moisture method. The chemistry surrounds and crystallises the soil, which you then vacuum away. Far less water goes in, so the room returns to service faster, though it's a lighter clean on heavy embedded soil. Think of it as dry time versus soil load, and choose deliberately. Resist quoting a fixed dry time. Explain the trade-off, manage airflow on site, and let conditions govern drying. Next, we'll look at the chemistry that supports both methods.

briefs.lessonTitle: Pre-spray, spotting and protection with the CCW/Actichem range
briefs.script: Good carpet cleaning is as much about the right chemistry as the right machine. The CCW and Actichem range gives you a practical Australian product set for pre-spray and extraction, targeted spotting, and protection. Whatever you reach for, the rule stays the same. Read the Safety Data Sheet before use, and dilute strictly to the product label. For pre-spray and extraction you have options like Clean Force, Extracta Pro, the Oxyboost booster, the Foamex line, and Hydramaster Release with Oxybreak. Match the product to the soil and fibre you identified at pre-inspection. Spots need targeted chemistry, not a blanket approach, so the range covers browning, general spotting, chewing gum, and red stains. Work spots before a general clean, test in an inconspicuous area first, and follow each product's SDS. Fibre Shield is the protector, applied to help the carpet resist soiling after cleaning. Remember, citing an AP-code only tells you which sheet to open. The SDS carries the hazards, PPE and handling, and the label carries the dilution. That matters more when you're working around children and staff. Next, we'll put this into a low-disruption method for occupied rooms.

briefs.lessonTitle: Low-disruption method and equipment for classrooms and care rooms
briefs.script: Cleaning carpet in a classroom or care room is a logistics problem as much as a cleaning one. The space has to come back into use quickly, and you're often working near children and staff. For occupied rooms, an encapsulation approach using an orbital scrubber such as the Polystar thirteen-inch orbital encapsulation scrubber is a strong fit. Because it's low-moisture, the carpet returns to service faster than after a full extraction, and you vacuum up the encapsulated soil once the chemistry has done its work. Even low-moisture work leaves the carpet damp for a period, so manage drying actively with airflow and ventilation rather than promising a fixed time. While it's damp, it's a slip and access risk. Trailing cords and wet floors are the two hazards most likely to cause a problem around children, so route your cords, keep gear out of reach, and isolate and sign any wet floor. Sequence rooms so each dries and reopens while you move on, and fit deeper work into holiday programs. Work within the site's own child-safe rules and induction. Next, a short knowledge check to confirm the key points.

briefs.lessonTitle: Knowledge check: carpet cleaning
briefs.script: That brings us to a short self-check to confirm the key points from this module. There are five questions, and they cover the status of S100 as a standard of care rather than law, the trade-off between hot water extraction and encapsulation, why encapsulation suits occupied rooms, how to use the Actichem products safely, and good practice when cleaning around children. Take a moment to attempt each one and choose your answer before you reveal the rationale. There's no pressure here. It's simply a chance to check what's landed. When you're ready, work through the questions on screen, then move on to the next module.

briefs.lessonTitle: Hard-floor and grout soil: tools, brushes and chemistry
briefs.script: In this lesson we look at why tile and grout behave like two different surfaces. The tile face is usually dense and largely sealed, so soil sits on top and rinses away easily. Grout lines, though, are porous. They soak up greasy and particulate soil, trap it below the surface, and hold the dirt that makes a floor look dingy even after the tiles look clean. So you are not just wiping a floor, you are lifting soil out of an absorbent material that resists casual mopping. Effective cleaning needs three things together: the right chemistry to loosen and suspend the soil, mechanical agitation to break it free from inside the grout, and proper rinse and recovery to lift the suspended soil away rather than redepositing it. For agitation, the Dirty Grout Demon brushes handle detail and edges, while the Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head suits production cleaning across an open floor. Greasy soils are typically tackled with alkaline chemistry, such as the Actichem hard-floor range. Always treat the SDS and technical guidance as the authority on dilution, hazards and safe use. Next, we put this into a repeatable method.

briefs.lessonTitle: Method, dwell and rinse — and slip resistance after cleaning (AS 4663)
briefs.script: Now let's turn the principles into a repeatable method, because a reliable result comes from working the same sequence every time, not from working harder. Follow a sound order: pre-wet, apply the solution, allow it to dwell, agitate, rinse, then recover. Pre-wetting stops a dry, porous grout line from drinking up your solution unevenly. Let it dwell for the time the product label and SDS or TDS specify, so the chemistry loosens the soil. Then agitate, rinse thoroughly to flush soil out of the grout, and recover the dirty solution so it does not dry back as a film. Residue is the most common reason a floor looks good wet and disappointing dry, so apply only as much product as you can rinse and recover. Cleaning can also change a floor's slip resistance. For a floor already in service, slip resistance is measured under AS 4663. Remember that it measures existing surfaces only; it does not set cure, dry or re-entry times. While a floor is wet it is a hazard, so isolate the area and use signage until it is dry. Then record your method, and move on to the knowledge check.

briefs.lessonTitle: Knowledge check: tile & grout
briefs.script: Before we wrap up the module, here is a short knowledge check to confirm what you have taken from these two lessons. Everything you need has already been covered, so there is nothing new to learn here. Take a moment to think through each question on screen and choose the answer you believe is correct. Focus on the key ideas we have worked through: why porous grout traps soil while the denser tile face rinses clean, matching the right tool to the job, and what the Safety Data Sheet does and does not tell you. Keep in mind, too, what AS 4663 actually measures, and the sound order of the cleaning method from pre-wet through to recovery. Work through each question at your own pace, then review the answers and the rationale provided so you understand not just the correct choice but the reasoning behind it. When you are confident, you are ready to move on to the next module.

briefs.lessonTitle: The strip-and-seal process, end to end
briefs.script: Let's walk through vinyl strip-and-seal from start to finish, because this is one of the highest-risk jobs in commercial floor care. It uses caustic chemistry, it leaves a wet, slippery surface, and it depends on getting the sequence right. At a high level, the generic process runs like this. You strip the old finish. You rinse and neutralise. You dry the floor. Then you apply multiple thin coats of sealer and finish, drying between each coat. Each stage prepares the surface for the next, so rushing or skipping a step undermines everything that follows. We can map that to a real Australian system from the Actichem catalogue — stripping with Instastrip or Power Strip, prepping with Pre-Seal, sealing with Instaseal, and finishing with Instagloss. Every one of those products has a current Safety Data Sheet and Technical Data Sheet you must obtain and read first. The dilutions, coat counts and drying are product-specific — they live in those documents, never in memory. In a school or childcare setting, plan and isolate the area for the full job, usually within a holiday program. Next, we cover caustic stripper safety.

briefs.lessonTitle: Caustic stripper safety: PPE, ventilation, isolation and signage
briefs.script: The strippers used in this work are typically alkaline, or caustic, and that single fact drives the whole safety approach. Caustic chemistry can burn skin and eyes, give off vapours, and the wet stripped floor becomes a serious slip and access hazard. Anchor your controls to your WHS duties and to that stripper's Safety Data Sheet. Under the model WHS framework, a PCBU that handles hazardous chemicals must keep a current SDS for each one, accessible to workers, with a hazardous chemicals register, and any decanted containers correctly labelled. Note that Victoria runs its own OHS laws, so check with your state regulator. For products like Actichem Instastrip or Power Strip, the SDS is your source for the right gloves, eye protection, ventilation, spill control and first-aid response. Read it before the job, not during an incident. The wet or curing floor is a hazard in its own right, so isolate the area and sign it clearly, and keep people out the whole time it is wet or curing. In schools and childcare, children may not heed a sign, so physical isolation and out-of-hours scheduling protect them far better. Next, sealing and finishing.

briefs.lessonTitle: Sealing and finishing — cure & re-entry come from the SDS/TDS, not guesswork
briefs.script: Once the floor is stripped, rinsed, neutralised and fully dry, you build the new surface back up with coats of sealer and then finish. The craft is in applying thin, even coats and letting each one dry before the next goes down. The single most important rule of this lesson is this — do not rely on any fixed dry, cure, re-entry or buffing time. Those values are product-specific, and the only correct source is the product's Technical Data Sheet and Safety Data Sheet. Using a real Actichem system, the sealer base is Instaseal and the finish coats are Instagloss, with Pre-Seal setting up the surface beforehand. Thin coats dry and cure more predictably and give a more durable, even result. Applying a coat before the one beneath is ready traps solvent or water and weakens adhesion. Cure rates shift with temperature, humidity, airflow and coat thickness, so plan the job backwards from the TDS — count the coats, add the drying between them, allow full cure, and only then commit to a return date. That's why this work suits holiday or deep-clean programs. Next, we verify slip resistance.

briefs.lessonTitle: Verifying slip resistance to a target class (AS 4663)
briefs.script: A freshly sealed and finished floor can have a different slip resistance from the surface you started with. A glossy new finish may behave differently underfoot, especially when wet. So the job isn't truly finished until you've confirmed the floor still meets the slip-resistance target the site requires. There are two related Australian Standards here, doing two different jobs. AS 4586:2013 classifies new pedestrian surfaces — pre-installation testing of products before they go down. AS 4663:2013 measures existing surfaces, in service, on site. Because a strip-and-seal job produces an existing, in-service floor, AS 4663 is the correct basis to re-verify slip resistance afterwards. Be clear about its limits, though. AS 4663 measures and classifies the surface — it does not set cure or re-entry times. Re-entry timing comes from the product TDS and SDS. For the recommended target class for a location, refer to companion guidance such as SA HB 198 and the National Construction Code rather than carrying a fixed value in your head. After the finish has cured per the TDS, arrange measurement to AS 4663 and keep that result with your job records. Next, a short knowledge check.

briefs.lessonTitle: Knowledge check: strip & seal
briefs.script: Before you move on, let's pause and check the key points from this module. Take a moment to recall the correct order of the generic strip-and-seal process, and where the right dry, cure and re-entry times actually come from. Think about the nature of vinyl strippers and what that means for your controls on site. Bring to mind which Australian Standard is the correct basis for verifying the finished, in-service floor, and what that standard does and does not tell you. And picture the right control while a stripped or freshly recoated floor is still wet or curing, especially in a childcare setting. When you're ready, work through the questions on screen and choose the best answer for each. Take your time — the goal here is understanding the safety and process logic, not just ticking boxes. Once you've completed the check, you'll have finished the strip-and-seal module.

briefs.lessonTitle: Pressure-washing technique, surfaces and pressures
briefs.script: Pressure washing looks simple, but on a school or childcare site it rewards good judgement. The same machine that strips gum off a path can gouge soft pavers, etch concrete, or blow render off a wall. The skill is matching the pressure and nozzle to the surface in front of you. So start by reading that surface. Sound concrete takes more than older or decorative finishes. Sand-jointed pavers can pit or lose their jointing sand. Soft-fall, rubberised play surfaces, line-marking, timber and anything sealed are easily damaged, so use the lowest practical pressure, or a different method. If you're unsure, test a small out-of-the-way patch first. With nozzles, work with the widest fan that still does the job, keep it moving, and hold a steady distance and angle. Treat the equipment as a real injury hazard. High-pressure water can break skin, so never point the lance at anyone, and never clear a blockage with the trigger live. Plan the work so children are nowhere near it, cordon the zone off, and hand back only a dry, safe surface. Next, we look at where all that wash water goes.

briefs.lessonTitle: “The drain is just for rain”: capturing run-off and trade-waste to sewer
briefs.script: Most sites have two drainage systems, and confusing them is an easy way to cause real harm. Stormwater drains, the grates and pits around the carpark and paths, are built to carry rain, and in most areas that water flows untreated straight to creeks, rivers and the sea. As the regulators put it, the drain is just for rain. The sewer is the other system, and it goes to a treatment plant. Pressure-washing run-off isn't clean. It carries detergents, grease, grit, paint flakes and gum, so it must not enter stormwater. The practical answer is to confine the area, collect the wash water, and direct it to sewer. Use booms, socks or bunding to contain it, cover nearby stormwater pits, and recover the water with a wet vacuum. Getting it to sewer isn't as simple as tipping it down a gully, though. Discharging trade wastewater needs prior written approval, a trade-waste agreement, from the water authority, in place before you discharge. A general environmental duty also applies. Any penalty figures you hear are illustrative and jurisdiction-specific. When in doubt, confirm the specifics with your local water authority and EPA, because the rules are local. Next, a short knowledge check.

briefs.lessonTitle: Knowledge check: pressure washing
briefs.script: Let's pause and check the key points from this module before you move on. This is a short self-check, and you'll work through it on screen, so I won't read the options out. As you go, keep the core ideas in mind. On surfaces, think about how you'd protect sand-jointed pavers and when you'd test a patch first. On timing, remember why outdoor pressure washing belongs in a vacated, cordoned-off area on a childcare site. On drainage, recall what the drain is just for rain really means, and why contaminated wash water must stay out of stormwater. On discharge, keep in mind what approval you need before sending trade wastewater to sewer. And if someone quotes an exact penalty figure, remember to treat it as illustrative and jurisdiction-specific, and verify it against the current Act. Take your time, choose one answer for each, then check yourself against the worked answers provided. When you're confident, we'll move on to winning and servicing the contract.

briefs.lessonTitle: Scheduling around occupancy, term breaks and deep-clean windows
briefs.script: Let's talk about scheduling, because on a school or childcare site the timing is part of the service. Your work splits into two kinds. Routine work is the light, frequent cleaning that keeps a floor presentable and safe. Periodic work is the heavy stuff: hard-floor strip and seal, and carpet hot water extraction. Those periodic jobs usually go into deep-clean or holiday windows, not the daily routine. That pattern shows up in published programs like School Infrastructure New South Wales, but treat it as directional and always check the actual contract spec. The reason is simple. A freshly stripped floor is wet and curing, which is a slip and access hazard, and extraction leaves moisture that needs to dry. You can't run either through a corridor full of children. When you must work in an occupied centre, lean on three levers: sequence room by room so there's always usable space, build in realistic drying time, and barrier off and sign any wet or curing floor. Map your periodic program to term breaks early, and confirm dates and access with the site rather than relying on assumed frequencies.

briefs.lessonTitle: Compliance & documentation: SDS register, WWC checks, NQS QA2 and school cleaning standards
briefs.script: A school or childcare contract assumes a baseline of paperwork before you arrive. Let's walk through it at a high level, pointing to the authority for each item. None of this replaces the contract's own rules. First, the SDS register. If you use, handle or store hazardous chemicals, you have duties under work health and safety law. Keep a current safety data sheet for each one, accessible to workers on site. A sheet is treated as current when it's not more than five years old and states its last-reviewed date. Note that Victoria runs its own framework, so check with your state regulator. Next, Working With Children checks. Child-related work generally requires one, they're administered separately in each state and territory, and they're usually not auto-transferable. A check may also not be the only screening a site wants; many add their own induction. For childcare, your methods should support National Quality Standard Quality Area two on health and safety, though that's the centre's obligation, not yours to certify. And school cleaning standards give directional frequencies; the binding detail is the current contract spec.

briefs.lessonTitle: Capstone: build your child-safe floor-care service offer
briefs.script: This is where the whole course comes together into one deliverable: a one-page, child-safe floor-care offer you can put to a school or childcare client. It's not a brochure. It's a clear statement of how you'll deliver safe, compliant work. Build it section by section. State your method per surface. For carpet, name hot water extraction for deeper soil, or encapsulation as a low-moisture option that returns a space to use faster. For vinyl, set out strip, rinse and neutralise, dry, then thin sealer coats, making clear that dilution, coat counts and re-entry steps come from each product's SDS and technical data sheet. List your chemicals with a current SDS, within five years, in an accessible register. Explain how product choices support Quality Area two, as alignment of intent, not certification. Commit to re-verifying slip resistance after sealing on the basis of AS 4663, which measures in-service surfaces only and doesn't set re-entry times. Show how you'll keep pressure-wash run-off out of stormwater, confining and collecting it to sewer under a trade-waste agreement. Place periodic work in low-occupancy windows. List your documentation. Then take the single next action this whole course has been driving toward: put a compliant, child-safe floor-care offer to a school or childcare client.

briefs.lessonTitle: Knowledge check: the contract
briefs.script: Time for a quick knowledge check on winning and servicing a floor-care contract. There are five questions on screen, and I won't read the options aloud. As you work through them, think about why periodic work like strip and seal belongs in a low-occupancy window, when a safety data sheet counts as current, how Working With Children checks vary by state and may not be the only screening a site asks for, which standard re-verifies slip resistance on a finished in-service floor, and where pressure-wash run-off should go. Choose your answer for each, then check it against the explanation below. If any feel shaky, that's your signal to revisit that lesson before you quote.
